meta.title: SF-SPC-4: Warehouses, Sharing and Semi-Structured Data

questions.stem: An application developer runs `ALTER TABLE large_table CLUSTER BY (customer_segment, order_date)`. The table already has 500 million rows. What happens immediately after this command?

options.text: The clustering key is registered but no physical reclustering occurs immediately — Snowflake's background Automatic Clustering service gradually recluster the table over time.
options.explanation: ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY registers the clustering key metadata immediately, but the physical reorganization of existing micro-partitions is performed asynchronously by the Automatic Clustering background service over time. New data inserted after the key is defined will also be clustered. Existing data is reclustered gradually.

options.text: The command fails unless the table is empty.
options.explanation: The physical reclustering is asynchronous and does not block reads.

options.text: Snowflake creates a new sorted copy of the table and swaps it atomically.
options.explanation: The command succeeds on tables with existing data.

options.text: Snowflake immediately reshuffles all 500 million rows into the new clustering order, blocking reads until complete.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not create a full copy of the table.

questions.stem: An application developer runs `CREATE TABLE orders_backup CLONE orders AT(OFFSET => -3600)`. What does this create?

options.text: A backup table containing only the rows that changed in the last 3600 seconds.
options.explanation: The clone contains the complete table state at that historical point, not just changed rows.

options.text: This is invalid syntax — CLONE does not support AT() time travel.
options.explanation: This is a one-time DDL statement, not a scheduled job.

options.text: A zero-copy clone of the orders table as it existed exactly 3600 seconds (1 hour) ago.
options.explanation: Snowflake allows zero-copy clones to be created from a historical Time Travel point using AT() or BEFORE() syntax. The resulting table reflects the state of the source table at the specified offset (3600 seconds = 1 hour ago). This is useful for creating point-in-time snapshots.

options.text: A scheduled backup job that runs every 3600 seconds.
options.explanation: CLONE with AT() is valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: Which system-defined Snowflake role is responsible for managing user and role creation across the account?

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: USERADMIN is the system-defined role in Snowflake specifically designed for managing users and roles. It has CREATE USER and CREATE ROLE privileges at the account level.

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN manages security policies and can also manage users/roles but is a higher-level role.

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: ACCOUNTADMIN is the highest-privilege role that encompasses all capabilities but is not the designated user management role for daily operations.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: SYSADMIN manages databases and warehouse objects, not users/roles by default.

questions.stem: A team uses Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE on an S3 external stage. A new file arrives in S3. What is the complete sequence of events that causes the file to be loaded?

options.text: The user manually runs REFRESH SNOWPIPE to trigger ingestion of new files.
options.explanation: Snowpipe does not poll S3 on a timer; it is event-driven via SQS.

options.text: Snowflake polls S3 every minute, detects the new file, and triggers ingestion.
options.explanation: Azure Event Grid is the equivalent mechanism on Azure, not on AWS S3.

options.text: Azure Event Grid sends a notification to Snowflake when the file arrives.
options.explanation: The entire pipeline is automated when AUTO_INGEST = TRUE; manual REFRESH is used when AUTO_INGEST is false.

options.text: S3 sends an event notification to an SQS queue; Snowpipe reads the SQS message, retrieves the file from S3, and loads it into the target table.
options.explanation: Snowpipe's AUTO_INGEST on AWS works via S3 event notifications. When a file is written to the monitored S3 prefix, S3 sends an event notification to an AWS SQS queue that was configured when the Snowpipe was created. Snowflake's Snowpipe service continuously monitors this SQS queue, picks up the notification, retrieves the file from S3, and loads it into the target table — typically within a minute.

questions.stem: An application developer creates a zero-copy clone of a 1 TB production table. Initially, how much additional storage does the clone consume?

options.text: Depends on the size of the virtual warehouse used for cloning.
options.explanation: Zero-copy clone creates no physical data copy at clone time.

options.text: 0 bytes — the clone shares all micro-partitions with the original until data is modified.
options.explanation: Zero-copy cloning in Snowflake does not copy any data at creation time. The clone is a metadata operation — the clone's metadata points to the same underlying micro-partitions as the original table. Additional storage is only incurred when either the original or the clone has data modified, causing Snowflake to write new micro-partitions for the changed data (copy-on-write).

options.text: 1 TB — a full copy of all micro-partitions is made.
options.explanation: No delta compression or partial copy is stored.

options.text: 500 GB — Snowflake stores a compressed delta of the original data.
options.explanation: Cloning is a metadata operation that does not use a virtual warehouse.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following are valid Snowflake materialized view limitations? (Select TWO)

options.text: Materialized views can only be created on tables in the same schema.
options.explanation: Materialized view definitions can join tables across schemas (within the same database), though cross-database joins have limitations.

options.text: Materialized views are only available in the Enterprise edition and above.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. Materialized views in Snowflake do not support window functions such as ROW_NUMBER() or RANK() because these are non-deterministic relative to partition changes and would make incremental maintenance impractical. Materialized views are also an Enterprise edition feature and are not available on the Standard edition.

options.text: Materialized views cannot contain window functions (e.g., ROW_NUMBER(), RANK()).
Materialized views can only be queried by users with the ACCOUNTADMIN role.
options.explanation: Materialized views can be queried by any user with appropriate SELECT privileges — ACCOUNTADMIN is not required.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants all future tables created in `mydb.public` to automatically have SELECT granted to the `ANALYST` role. Which command achieves this?

options.text: `ALTER SCHEMA mydb.public SET DEFAULT GRANT SELECT TO ROLE analyst;`
options.explanation: ON ALL TABLES applies only to existing tables and does not affect future tables.

options.text: `GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.public TO ROLE analyst;`
options.explanation: `GRANT ... ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ...` is the Snowflake feature that automatically grants the specified privilege to new tables created in the schema after the grant is issued. This is the future grants mechanism.

options.text: `CREATE POLICY auto_grant AS GRANT SELECT ON NEW TABLES TO ROLE analyst;`
options.explanation: ALTER SCHEMA does not have a DEFAULT GRANT syntax.

options.text: `GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.public TO ROLE analyst;`
options.explanation: CREATE POLICY with auto-grant syntax does not exist in Snowflake.

questions.stem: When examining a query profile in Snowflake, a developer sees that the `TableScan` node shows `Bytes scanned: 500 GB` but `Bytes spilled to local storage: 200 GB`. What does 'bytes spilled to local storage' indicate?

options.text: 200 GB of query results were written to an internal Snowflake table as overflow.
options.explanation: Spilling does not write to user tables.

options.text: Snowflake moved 200 GB of data to the result cache for future query reuse.
options.explanation: Pruned data is not scanned at all — spilling refers to intermediate data written mid-execution.

options.text: The query read 200 GB of redundant data that was pruned after the initial scan.
options.explanation: Spilling is not related to the result cache.

options.text: The virtual warehouse ran out of in-memory space during query execution and wrote intermediate data to local SSD, degrading performance.
options.explanation: 'spilled to local storage' in the Snowflake query profile means that the warehouse's in-memory capacity was insufficient to hold all intermediate query data (e.g., hash tables for joins or sort buffers), causing Snowflake to spill overflow to the warehouse nodes' local SSD. This is significantly slower than in-memory operations and indicates the warehouse may be undersized for the query's memory requirements.

questions.stem: A table has a VARIANT column `tags` that contains JSON arrays like `["sales","vip","premium"]`. An application developer wants to query each tag as a separate row. Which Snowflake function should be used?

options.text: UNNEST(tags)
options.explanation: SPLIT works on string values and returns an ARRAY — it does not create rows.

options.text: ARRAY_TO_ROWS(tags)
options.explanation: UNNEST is a PostgreSQL/BigQuery function not supported in Snowflake.

options.text: FLATTEN(INPUT => tags)
options.explanation: FLATTEN is the Snowflake table function used to expand VARIANT arrays (and nested structures) into multiple rows. Used with LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => tags), each element of the JSON array becomes a separate row with columns like VALUE, INDEX, and KEY.

options.text: SPLIT(tags, ',')
options.explanation: ARRAY_TO_ROWS is not a valid Snowflake function.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of Snowpipe in Snowflake?

options.text: To enable continuous, near-real-time automated data ingestion from a stage into a Snowflake table.
options.explanation: Snowpipe is Snowflake's continuous data ingestion service. It monitors a stage (typically triggered by cloud storage notifications like S3 event notifications or SQS messages) and automatically loads new files as they arrive, providing near-real-time ingestion without managing scheduled batch jobs.

options.text: To provide a SQL interface for piping data between Snowflake tables in real time.
options.explanation: Snowpipe does not pipe data between Snowflake tables — that is done with INSERT/MERGE or streams/tasks.

options.text: To establish a network pipe (VPN) between an on-premises data center and Snowflake.
options.explanation: Snowpipe is a data ingestion service, not a network pipe.

options.text: To schedule batch COPY INTO operations at defined time intervals.
options.explanation: Snowpipe is event-driven, not time-interval scheduled — scheduled batch loads use task-triggered COPY INTO.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake architectural layer handles query compilation and optimization before sending work to the virtual warehouse?

options.text: Cloud Services layer
options.explanation: The Cloud Services layer is responsible for query parsing, optimization, and compilation. It uses the metadata cache (containing table statistics and micro-partition metadata) to generate an efficient execution plan before dispatching work to the virtual warehouse.

options.text: Compute layer (virtual warehouse)
options.explanation: The compute layer (virtual warehouse) executes the already-compiled query plan but does not compile it.

options.text: Network ingress layer
options.explanation: The storage layer persists micro-partitioned data in cloud object storage.

options.text: Storage layer
options.explanation: 'network ingress layer' is not a Snowflake architectural component.

questions.stem: Which SQL statement creates a Snowflake table UDF (UDTF) that returns a table of values?

options.text: `CREATE TABLE FUNCTION split_values(input VARCHAR) AS SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(input, ","));`
options.explanation: CREATE TABLE FUNCTION is not valid Snowflake syntax — it is CREATE FUNCTION with RETURNS TABLE.

options.text: `CREATE FUNCTION split_values(input VARCHAR) RETURNS TABLE (val VARCHAR) LANGUAGE SQL AS 'SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(input, ","))';`
options.explanation: Snowflake UDTFs (User-Defined Table Functions) use `CREATE FUNCTION ... RETURNS TABLE (column_list) LANGUAGE SQL AS` syntax. This is the correct syntax for creating a table-valued function.

options.text: `CREATE PROCEDURE split_values(input VARCHAR) RETURNS TABLE AS BEGIN SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(input, ",")); END;`
options.explanation: RETURNS TABLE in a stored procedure context is used for procedure results, and the syntax shown is not the correct UDF pattern.

options.text: `CREATE VIEW split_values AS SELECT value FROM TABLE(SPLIT_TO_TABLE(:input, ","));`
options.explanation: Views are static definitions and do not accept parameters.

questions.stem: An application developer is writing a stored procedure that needs to query tables the caller does not have direct SELECT privileges on. The procedure owner has SELECT on those tables. Which stored procedure property allows the procedure to run with the owner's privileges instead of the caller's?

options.text: EXECUTE AS CALLER
options.explanation: WITH GRANT OPTION is a privilege grant option, not a stored procedure execution context.

options.text: SECURITY DEFINER
options.explanation: EXECUTE AS CALLER makes the procedure run with the caller's privileges, which would fail if the caller lacks SELECT on the tables.

options.text: EXECUTE AS OWNER
options.explanation: `EXECUTE AS OWNER` (the default for stored procedures in Snowflake) means the procedure runs with the privileges of the procedure's owner role, not the role of the user calling the procedure. This allows the procedure to access objects the caller cannot access directly — similar to SECURITY DEFINER in PostgreSQL.

options.text: WITH GRANT OPTION
options.explanation: SECURITY DEFINER is not Snowflake syntax — the equivalent is EXECUTE AS OWNER.

questions.reference: [Understanding caller's rights and owner's rights stored procedures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/stored-procedure/stored-procedures-rights)

questions.stem: A company needs to ensure that sensitive column values in a shared table are masked differently based on whether the querying user is an internal employee or an external consumer. Which Snowflake feature combination achieves this?

options.text: Column-level encryption using customer-managed keys with role-specific decryption.
options.explanation: Row access policies filter rows, not column values — combining with a secure view would work but doesn't specifically address column value masking per consumer type.

options.text: Two separate tables — one with full data for internal users and one pre-masked for external consumers.
options.explanation: Column-level encryption with per-role decryption is not a native Snowflake feature.

options.text: Row Access Policy filtering on IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() combined with a secure view.
options.explanation: Maintaining two separate tables adds operational complexity and data synchronization risk.

options.text: A Dynamic Data Masking policy that evaluates CURRENT_ROLE() or IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() to return full or masked values.
options.explanation: Dynamic Data Masking policies can include conditional logic using CURRENT_ROLE() or IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() to return the full value for trusted internal roles and a masked value for external consumer roles. Since masking policies apply transparently at query time, the same shared table serves both audiences with appropriate data visibility.

questions.stem: An application developer uses `CREATE TABLE dev_orders CLONE prod_orders`. The cloned table is used for development testing and has many rows deleted and updated. Which statement about storage costs is TRUE?

options.text: The clone and original share all storage costs; no additional storage is ever charged.
options.explanation: Modifications to either the clone or original do create new micro-partitions that are charged.

options.text: Deleted rows in the clone reduce storage costs for the provider immediately.
options.explanation: Deleted rows in Snowflake are still stored until their Time Travel and Fail-Safe periods expire — deletion does not immediately free storage.

options.text: The clone immediately doubles storage costs because a full copy is made.
options.explanation: Zero-copy clone creates no duplicate of unchanged data at clone time.

options.text: Storage charges accumulate only for micro-partitions that have been modified in either the clone or the original — shared unmodified partitions are counted once.
options.explanation: Snowflake's zero-copy clone uses copy-on-write semantics. Micro-partitions that are unchanged in both the clone and the original are shared and counted once in storage. New micro-partitions are created and charged separately only when data is modified in either the clone or the original. So storage costs increase incrementally as changes accumulate, not immediately.

questions.stem: An engineer runs `GET @my_stage/results.csv file:///tmp/results.csv`. What does this command do?

options.text: Uploads the local file `/tmp/results.csv` to the Snowflake internal stage.
options.explanation: Uploading uses PUT, not GET.

options.text: Validates that the file in the stage matches the local file checksum.
options.explanation: GET downloads to local file system, not to another cloud storage location.

options.text: Downloads the file `results.csv` from the internal stage to the local path `/tmp/results.csv`.
options.explanation: The GET command downloads a file from a Snowflake internal (or external) stage to the local file system. The syntax is `GET @<stage>/<path> file://<local_path>`. This is the complement to PUT, which uploads files.

options.text: Copies the file from the internal stage to an external S3 bucket.
options.explanation: GET is a data transfer command, not a validation command.

questions.stem: An engineer runs: `COPY INTO sales FROM @my_stage/data.csv.gz ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE'`. The file contains 1000 rows and 50 rows fail validation. What is the result?

options.text: Snowflake loads all 1000 rows and marks the 50 invalid ones with NULL values.
options.explanation: ON_ERROR = 'ABORT_STATEMENT' (the default) would reject the entire load, but CONTINUE overrides this.

options.text: 950 valid rows are loaded; the 50 invalid rows are skipped and errors are recorded in the copy history.
options.explanation: `ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE'` instructs Snowflake to skip rows that fail to parse or validate and continue processing the remaining rows. The 950 valid rows are inserted into the table. Details about the 50 failed rows are available in the COPY_HISTORY or load history.

options.text: The command fails because ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE' is not a valid COPY INTO option.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not insert malformed rows with NULLs under CONTINUE — it skips them.

options.text: The entire load is rejected and no rows are inserted due to the 50 errors.
options.explanation: ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE' is a valid and commonly used COPY INTO option.

questions.stem: A team is migrating an Oracle PL/SQL pipeline to Snowflake. Which TWO languages can be used to write stored procedures in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.text: Python (using Snowpark for Python)
options.explanation: and **D** are correct. Snowflake stored procedures support Snowflake Scripting (the native procedural SQL language), Python (via Snowpark), JavaScript, Java, and Scala.

options.text: PL/SQL (native Oracle compatibility)
options.explanation: Snowflake does not support Oracle PL/SQL natively — Snowflake Scripting is the procedural SQL alternative.

options.text: Go (Golang)
options.explanation: Go (Golang) is not a supported language for Snowflake stored procedures as of the current release.

options.text: Snowflake Scripting (Snowpark procedural SQL)

questions.stem: A team is designing a large fact table that is frequently queried by date range and region. Which TWO Snowflake features work TOGETHER to minimize data scanned for these queries? (Select TWO)

options.text: Multi-cluster warehouse to run more parallel queries
options.explanation: Multi-cluster adds concurrency, not scan efficiency for a single query.

options.text: CLUSTER BY (sale_date, region) to co-locate relevant micro-partitions
options.explanation: and **D** are correct and are inherently linked. Defining a CLUSTER BY key on (sale_date, region) ensures that data is physically co-located in micro-partitions by those columns. Snowflake's automatic partition pruning then uses the min/max metadata stored per micro-partition to skip partitions that cannot contain qualifying rows for a given date range and region filter. Together, clustering and pruning dramatically reduce the bytes scanned.

options.text: Increasing warehouse size to scan partitions faster
options.explanation: A larger warehouse scans the same number of partitions faster but does not reduce the number of partitions scanned.

options.text: Automatic micro-partition pruning based on min/max metadata in clustered partitions

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider shares a database with two consumer accounts. Consumer A is a paying Snowflake customer. Consumer B is a non-Snowflake customer. How does Consumer B access the shared data?

options.text: Consumer B receives a nightly data export via email in CSV format.
options.explanation: Snowflake data sharing is live and real-time — data is not exported or emailed.

options.text: Consumer B accesses the data via the provider's internal Snowflake account using guest user credentials.
options.explanation: Reader accounts are specifically designed so that non-Snowflake customers do not need their own full Snowflake account.

options.text: The provider creates a reader account for Consumer B; the provider pays for the reader account's compute credits.
options.explanation: Snowflake provides 'reader accounts' specifically for sharing data with non-Snowflake customers. The data provider creates and manages the reader account; the provider is billed for any compute credits the reader account uses to query the shared data.

options.text: Consumer B must upgrade to a Snowflake account before they can access any shared data.
options.explanation: Guest credentials inside the provider's account are not the mechanism for external sharing.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to allow a third-party ETL tool to connect using key-pair authentication. Which parameter is set on the Snowflake user object to register the public key?

options.text: ALTER USER etl_user SET PASSWORD = '<public_key>';`
options.explanation: PASSWORD is for password-based authentication, not key-pair; setting the public key as a password would not enable key-pair auth.

options.text: `GRANT KEY PAIR TO USER etl_user USING '<public_key_value>';`
options.explanation: API_KEY is not a valid Snowflake user parameter.

options.text: `ALTER USER etl_user SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key_value>';`
options.explanation: Key-pair authentication in Snowflake is configured by setting the `RSA_PUBLIC_KEY` parameter on the user object using `ALTER USER ... SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY`. The ETL tool then authenticates using the corresponding private key.

options.text: `ALTER USER etl_user SET API_KEY = '<public_key_value>';`
options.explanation: GRANT KEY PAIR is not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A Snowflake table has 1 million rows. An application developer runs `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM large_table`. Which cache is MOST LIKELY used to serve this query without involving the virtual warehouse?

options.text: Query result cache
options.explanation: The local disk cache is on the warehouse nodes and requires the warehouse to be active.

options.text: Local disk cache
Metadata cache
options.explanation: `SELECT COUNT(*)` on a table is a metadata-only operation that Snowflake can satisfy from the Cloud Services layer's metadata cache, which stores row counts and other statistics about every table. No virtual warehouse is required. B is also a possibility if the exact query was run before within 24 hours, but the metadata cache specifically enables COUNT(*) to be answered without ever engaging the warehouse.

options.text: External cache
options.explanation: 'external cache' is not a Snowflake concept.

questions.stem: A Snowflake Enterprise account has a table with `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 30`. An administrator runs `ALTER TABLE orders SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0`. What are the implications?

options.text: Time Travel is disabled for future changes; existing Time Travel data (up to 30 days) is gradually purged, and Fail-Safe still applies for 7 days after expiry.
options.explanation: Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for future changes to the table. Historical data that was accumulated during the 30-day retention period is not immediately purged — it is gradually expired according to when each micro-partition's time travel window closes. Fail-Safe (7 days) still applies after each micro-partition's Time Travel window expires.

options.text: Time Travel is disabled for the table; historical data from before the change is immediately purged.
options.explanation: Historical data is not immediately purged — it expires as the time windows close.

options.text: The command is rejected because you cannot reduce Time Travel below 1 day on Enterprise edition.
options.explanation: Reducing to 0 is allowed even on Enterprise edition.

options.text: Setting retention to 0 moves all historical data to Fail-Safe immediately for 30 days.
options.explanation: Fail-Safe is not activated immediately — it begins after each micro-partition's Time Travel window closes.

questions.stem: A Snowflake account is configured to use 'Standard' Time Travel (default 1-day retention). A table is dropped. How long does the administrator have to run UNDROP TABLE before the data is permanently unrecoverable by users?

options.text: 8 days (1 day Time Travel + 7 day Fail-Safe, both accessible via UNDROP)
options.explanation: Fail-Safe is NOT user-accessible.

options.text: 7 days (only Fail-Safe applies after a DROP TABLE)
options.explanation: UNDROP TABLE cannot access Fail-Safe data.

options.text: 1 day (Time Travel only; after expiry, data moves to Fail-Safe which users cannot access)
options.explanation: UNDROP TABLE uses Time Travel data. Once the 1-day Time Travel retention expires, the user can no longer run UNDROP TABLE — the data enters Fail-Safe, which is managed only by Snowflake Support and is NOT accessible to users via SQL commands. So the effective window for user recovery is 1 day.

options.text: 1 day (Time Travel expires, then Fail-Safe begins and is also user-accessible)
options.explanation: Fail-Safe begins after Time Travel expires, and neither period applies only to DROP TABLE specifically.

questions.stem: A data provider is on AWS us-east-1. A consumer account is on AWS eu-west-1. The provider creates a standard share. Can the consumer access the data?

options.text: Yes — Snowflake data sharing works across any cloud regions without restriction.
options.explanation: Standard shares are region-scoped.

options.text: No — standard shares only work within the same account.
options.explanation: Shares work across accounts within the same region, not just within the same account.

options.text: Yes, but the consumer must pay additional inter-region data transfer fees charged by Snowflake.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not directly charge consumers inter-region data transfer fees at the share level.

options.text: Data sharing across regions requires a Replication Group or Cross-Cloud Auto-Fulfillment; a simple share to a different region/cloud is limited.
options.explanation: A basic Snowflake share works natively only within the same cloud region. To share data across regions or cloud providers, the provider must either use Database Replication to replicate data to the consumer's region and then share from the replica, or use the Snowflake Marketplace's Cross-Cloud Auto-Fulfillment feature which handles replication automatically.

questions.reference: [Share data securely across regions and cloud platforms](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/secure-data-sharing-across-regions-plaforms)

questions.stem: Which TWO conditions must BOTH be true for Snowflake's result cache to serve a query result? (Select TWO — presented as a single-answer choice)

options.text: The query contains no WHERE clause AND uses only aggregate functions.
options.explanation: Any user can get the cached result, not only the original submitter.

options.text: The query runs on the same virtual warehouse AND the result was cached within 1 hour.
options.explanation: Result cache is not warehouse-specific and the TTL is 24 hours, not 1 hour.

options.text: The exact same SQL text is submitted AND the underlying tables' data has not changed since the cached result was produced.
options.explanation: Snowflake's result cache requires (1) the query text to be semantically identical to a previously cached query, and (2) the underlying table data (and metadata) to be unchanged since the cached result was created. The result cache persists for up to 24 hours and is available account-wide — different users on different warehouses can benefit from the same cached result.

options.text: The user submitting the query is the same user who originally ran it AND the warehouse size is the same.
options.explanation: Result cache applies to any query type, including those with WHERE clauses.

questions.stem: A data analyst runs a query and receives results. The exact same query is run again 10 minutes later without any changes to the underlying table. What is the expected behavior?

options.text: The query is blocked until the previous result expires.
options.explanation: The result cache prevents re-execution.

options.text: Snowflake returns the cached result instantly from the result cache, consuming no compute credits.
options.explanation: Snowflake's result cache persists query results for 24 hours. If the identical query is re-executed within that window and the underlying data has not changed, Snowflake serves the result directly from the result cache without engaging the virtual warehouse, consuming zero compute credits.

options.text: Snowflake uses the local disk cache on the warehouse nodes to speed up execution.
options.explanation: The local disk cache requires the warehouse to be active and does not eliminate compute use.

options.text: The warehouse re-executes the query, consuming compute credits.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not block repeat queries.

questions.stem: A Snowflake warehouse is set to AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 (seconds) and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. Multiple short queries arrive in rapid succession over a 2-hour period, with each burst lasting ~2 minutes followed by 5-minute idle gaps. What is the billing behavior?

options.text: The warehouse is billed continuously for 2 hours since it never fully suspends between bursts.
options.explanation: The 5-minute idle gaps exceed the 60-second auto-suspend threshold, so the warehouse does suspend repeatedly. B describes the mechanism correctly but is slightly imprecise about when suspension occurs — it is 60 seconds after the last query, not after each query individually.

options.text: The warehouse suspends after each 60-second idle period within the 5-minute gap, and each resume cycle incurs at least a 60-second minimum.
Credits are only billed for the 2-minute active bursts, and idle time is always free.
The warehouse suspends after each 60-second idle gap, and each resume incurs a new 60-second minimum charge.
options.explanation: Idle time before auto-suspend (the 60-second window) IS billed.

questions.stem: What is the difference between a named internal stage and a table stage in Snowflake?

options.text: Named internal stages support only CSV files; table stages support all file formats.
options.explanation: Table stages persist as long as the table exists and named stages are not session-specific.

options.text: A named internal stage is a separate Snowflake object that can be used to load data into any table; a table stage is automatically created with each table and can only be used to load that specific table.
options.explanation: Every Snowflake table has an automatically created table stage (@%table_name) that is tied to that specific table and can only load into it. Named internal stages are explicit Snowflake objects (created with CREATE STAGE) that can be used to load data into any table. Named stages offer more flexibility and are preferred for production pipelines.

options.text: Table stages require ACCOUNTADMIN privileges to use; named stages can be used by any role.
options.explanation: Both stage types support multiple file formats.

options.text: Table stages are permanent and shared across multiple tables; named stages are temporary and session-specific.
options.explanation: Table stages do not require ACCOUNTADMIN.

questions.stem: A data consumer has mounted a shared database from a provider. Which role in the CONSUMER account is required to CREATE DATABASE from a share?

options.text: The PUBLIC role
options.explanation: SYSADMIN does not inherently have IMPORT SHARE privilege — it must be explicitly granted.

options.text: SYSADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: PUBLIC has no import privileges.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN handles security policies and user management, not data share imports.

options.text: Any role with IMPORT SHARE privilege
options.explanation: To mount a share as a database in the consumer account, the role must have the IMPORT SHARE account-level privilege. By default, ACCOUNTADMIN has this privilege and can grant it to other roles.

questions.stem: An engineer creates a file format object: `CREATE FILE FORMAT my_json_fmt TYPE = 'JSON' STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE;`. What does STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY do?

options.text: Treats each element of a top-level JSON array as a separate row during loading, instead of loading the entire array as one VARIANT value.
options.explanation: `STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE` instructs Snowflake to parse a top-level JSON array and insert each array element as a separate row. Without this option, the entire JSON array would be loaded as a single VARIANT row. For example, `[{"id":1},{"id":2}]` would become two rows instead of one.

options.text: Removes the outermost JSON object keys, keeping only the values.
options.explanation: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY does not affect object keys.

options.text: Converts the JSON array to a CSV-formatted string before loading.
options.explanation: The data remains in JSON/VARIANT format — it is not converted to CSV.

options.text: Removes all nested arrays from the JSON before loading to prevent errors.
options.explanation: Nested arrays are preserved; only the outermost array wrapper is stripped.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to understand why a specific query was slow. Which Snowflake feature provides a visual breakdown of execution nodes, time spent at each step, and data flow within the query?

options.text: Query Profile in Snowsight
options.explanation: The Query Profile in Snowsight (the Snowflake web UI) provides an interactive, visual execution graph showing each operation node in the query plan, the time each node took, bytes scanned, rows produced, and spill information. It is the primary tool for diagnosing slow queries.

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
options.explanation: QUERY_HISTORY provides overall query metadata (total duration, credits, status) but not node-level execution detail.

options.text: EXPLAIN PLAN output
options.explanation: EXPLAIN PLAN shows the estimated execution plan before running but does not show actual runtime statistics.

options.text: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES
options.explanation: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES shows table metadata, not query execution details.

questions.stem: Which command shows the clustering information (Average Depth, Average Overlap Depth) for a Snowflake table to assess how well-clustered it is?

options.text: SHOW CLUSTERING KEYS IN TABLE orders;
options.explanation: DESCRIBE TABLE shows column definitions, not clustering statistics.

options.text: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2024-01-01';
options.explanation: EXPLAIN shows the query plan, not table clustering statistics.

options.text: DESCRIBE TABLE orders;
options.explanation: SHOW CLUSTERING KEYS shows what clustering keys are defined but not the clustering depth metrics.

options.text: SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('orders', '(order_date)');
options.explanation: SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION() is the Snowflake system function that returns detailed clustering statistics for a table, including Average Depth and Average Overlap Depth for a given column or set of columns. These metrics indicate how well the data is physically ordered relative to the specified key.

questions.stem: An engineer needs to unload query results from Snowflake to an S3 bucket in CSV format with gzip compression. Which command achieves this?

options.text: EXPORT TABLE sales TO 's3://mybucket/exports/' FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV) COMPRESSION = GZIP;
options.explanation: EXPORT TABLE is not valid Snowflake SQL.

options.text: COPY INTO 's3://mybucket/exports/' FROM (SELECT * FROM sales) FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV COMPRESSION = GZIP);
options.explanation: Snowflake uses COPY INTO to unload data from a table or query into an external stage or cloud storage location. The syntax `COPY INTO '<external_location>' FROM (SELECT ...)` with FILE_FORMAT options specifying TYPE = CSV and COMPRESSION = GZIP is the correct unloading syntax.

options.text: UNLOAD SELECT * FROM sales TO 's3://mybucket/exports/' FORMAT CSV GZIP;
options.explanation: UNLOAD is not a Snowflake command (it exists in other systems like Redshift).

options.text: PUT (SELECT * FROM sales) TO 's3://mybucket/exports/' COMPRESSION = GZIP;
options.explanation: PUT is for uploading local files to internal stages, not for exporting query results.

questions.stem: Which TWO functions can be used to safely handle missing keys in a VARIANT document in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.text: OBJECT_KEYS() — prevents errors by listing all available keys before access.
options.explanation: OBJECT_KEYS() lists keys but does not prevent access errors by itself.

options.text: TRY_PARSE_JSON() — returns NULL instead of an error if the input is not valid JSON.
options.explanation: and **D** are correct. TRY_PARSE_JSON() safely parses a JSON string and returns NULL if the input is malformed or NULL, rather than raising an error. GET(variant_col, 'key') returns NULL when the key does not exist in the VARIANT object — this is the safe behavior of both GET() and colon/bracket notation in Snowflake (accessing a missing key returns NULL VARIANT, not an error).

options.text: COALESCE() — automatically retries with an alternative key path if the first is missing.
options.explanation: COALESCE() evaluates expressions left to right and returns the first non-NULL, but it does not retry alternative key paths — both paths would still be evaluated.

options.text: GET() — returns NULL (not an error) when the key does not exist in the VARIANT.

questions.stem: A security team wants to audit all login attempts to their Snowflake account. Which Snowflake object or view provides this information?

options.text: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOGIN_HISTORY
options.explanation: `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOGIN_HISTORY` is a table function scoped to the current user's session and has a shorter retention period (7 days).

options.text: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SESSIONS
options.explanation: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SESSIONS provides active session information, not login history.

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
options.explanation: QUERY_HISTORY records SQL query execution, not authentication attempts.

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY
options.explanation: `ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY` provides a comprehensive, account-level view of all authentication attempts, including both successful and failed logins, across all users. It retains data for up to 365 days.

questions.stem: A table's Time Travel retention period is set to 7 days. After 7 days, the data enters Fail-Safe. How long does Fail-Safe last, and who can recover data from it?

options.text: Fail-Safe lasts 90 days and can be queried using the AT(FAIL_SAFE => TRUE) syntax.
options.explanation: Fail-Safe lasts 7 days (not 24 hours) and is not accessible via UNDROP TABLE.

options.text: Fail-Safe lasts 7 days and can only be recovered by Snowflake Support — it is not accessible via SQL.
options.explanation: Fail-Safe provides an additional 7-day retention period after Time Travel expires, but it is exclusively managed by Snowflake Support. Users cannot access Fail-Safe data via SQL commands — it is a last-resort disaster recovery mechanism for catastrophic failures.

options.text: Fail-Safe lasts indefinitely and is automatically converted to a long-term backup archive.
options.explanation: AT(FAIL_SAFE => TRUE) is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: Fail-Safe lasts 24 hours and can be recovered by the ACCOUNTADMIN role using UNDROP TABLE.
options.explanation: Fail-Safe has a fixed 7-day window and is not converted to long-term archives.

questions.stem: Which TWO Snowflake features are part of the Tri-Secret Secure encryption model? (Select TWO)

options.text: A hardware security module (HSM) physically located at the customer's premises
options.explanation: While Snowflake-managed keys are one component of standard encryption, the key differentiator of Tri-Secret Secure is the CUSTOMER-managed key component — just Snowflake-managed keys alone is not Tri-Secret Secure.

options.text: Customer-managed keys stored in the customer's cloud key management service (e.g., AWS KMS)
options.explanation: and **D** are correct. Tri-Secret Secure is a Business Critical edition feature that combines Snowflake's managed encryption keys with a customer-managed key (CMK) stored in the customer's own cloud KMS (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, or GCP Cloud KMS). Decrypting data requires both keys, meaning Snowflake cannot access data without the customer's key. Business Critical edition is required.

options.text: Snowflake-managed encryption keys for the account's data
options.explanation: Tri-Secret Secure uses cloud KMS, not on-premises HSMs.

options.text: Business Critical edition (required for Tri-Secret Secure)

questions.stem: An engineer needs to load data from an external S3 stage but wants each file's source name to be stored in the target table alongside the loaded data. Which COPY INTO feature provides this?

options.text: INCLUDE_METADATA option with the FILENAME metadata column.
options.explanation: INCLUDE_METADATA is not a standard COPY INTO option for capturing filenames.

options.text: The METADATA$FILENAME pseudocolumn available in COPY INTO SELECT.
options.explanation: Snowflake exposes pseudocolumns like METADATA$FILENAME and METADATA$FILE_ROW_NUMBER that are available when selecting from a stage. Using `COPY INTO table FROM (SELECT METADATA$FILENAME, $1, $2, ... FROM @my_stage)` allows the source file name to be captured and stored in a target column.

options.text: Adding a DEFAULT expression `DEFAULT CURRENT_FILE()` to the target table column.
options.explanation: CURRENT_FILE() is not a valid Snowflake default expression.

options.text: This is not possible in Snowflake — source file names cannot be captured during COPY INTO.
options.explanation: It is possible via the COPY INTO SELECT with METADATA$ pseudocolumns.

questions.stem: A virtual warehouse is configured with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 3 and MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 using the Standard scaling policy. The warehouse currently has 2 active clusters. The load drops significantly and there is no queuing. What happens?

options.text: The warehouse immediately scales down to 1 cluster.
options.explanation: Scale-down is not instantaneous.

options.text: The warehouse scales down to 0 clusters, suspending entirely.
options.explanation: Scale-down is automatic for multi-cluster warehouses.

options.text: The warehouse waits for 2-3 consecutive checks showing low load before scaling down a cluster.
options.explanation: Snowflake uses a conservative scale-down approach for multi-cluster warehouses. Even with the Standard policy, Snowflake performs consecutive load checks and only removes a cluster after confirming that reduced capacity is sustained — typically after 2-3 consecutive 1-minute checks show the cluster is underutilized. This prevents thrashing from brief load dips.

options.text: The warehouse scales down only when manually resized by an administrator.
options.explanation: MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 prevents the warehouse from scaling below 1 cluster.

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider wants to publish a dataset on the Snowflake Marketplace that allows consumers to instantly get access without requesting approval. Which listing type should they choose?

options.text: Auto-fulfillment Listing with request-and-approval workflow
options.explanation: Private listings are shared only with specific, named consumer accounts and require the provider to explicitly add each consumer.

options.text: Paid Listing with contract-based access
options.explanation: A request-and-approval workflow requires the provider to manually review and approve each access request — not instant.

options.text: Private Listing (direct share to specific accounts only)
options.explanation: Paid listings require consumers to agree to terms and complete a purchase, which is not instant auto-access.

options.text: Free Instant Listing with auto-fulfillment enabled
options.explanation: A Free Instant Listing on the Snowflake Marketplace with auto-fulfillment allows any Snowflake consumer to discover the listing and immediately get access to the data without a request/approval step. The data is shared live via Snowflake's native sharing mechanism.

questions.stem: A team wants to query data directly in S3 without loading it into a Snowflake table. Which TWO Snowflake features support querying files directly in cloud storage? (Select TWO)

options.text: Querying files in a stage using SELECT $1, $2 FROM @stage_name
options.explanation: and **D** are correct. External tables define a schema over files in an external stage, allowing them to be queried with standard SQL. Snowflake can also directly query staged files using `SELECT $1, $2, ... FROM @stage_name` syntax — the $N notation refers to positional columns in the file.

options.text: Secure views with EXTERNAL_TABLE = TRUE
options.explanation: Snowpipe is a continuous ingestion mechanism, not a direct query feature; DIRECT_QUERY is not a valid Snowpipe parameter.

options.text: Snowpipe with DIRECT_QUERY = TRUE
options.explanation: Secure views with EXTERNAL_TABLE = TRUE is not a valid Snowflake feature syntax.

options.text: External tables

questions.stem: Which TWO objects can be zero-copy cloned in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.text: Network policies
options.explanation: Network policies are account-level configuration objects and do not support cloning.

options.text: Databases (creating a clone of the entire database with all its schemas and tables)
options.explanation: and **D** are correct. Snowflake supports zero-copy cloning of tables, schemas, and databases. Cloning a database clones all its contained schemas and tables in a single metadata operation.

options.text: Virtual warehouses
options.explanation: Virtual warehouses are compute resources and cannot be cloned — they are created independently.

options.text: Tables (including their data)

questions.stem: A Snowflake provider wants to offer a dataset commercially with row-level usage metering (charging consumers per row they query). Which feature enables this?

options.text: Standard data shares with a custom billing report.
options.explanation: Standard shares have no built-in billing or metering mechanism.

options.text: Snowflake Marketplace Listings with Data Products and monetization enabled.
options.explanation: The Snowflake Marketplace supports monetized listings where providers can charge consumers for access to their data. Snowflake handles the billing and revenue split. Row-level or query-based metering is supported through the Snowflake Native App framework and monetization features in Marketplace listings.

options.text: Reader accounts with a fixed monthly fee applied by ACCOUNTADMIN.
options.explanation: Data Clean Rooms are for privacy-preserving collaborative analytics, not commercial data monetization.

options.text: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms.
options.explanation: Reader accounts bill the provider, not the consumer.

questions.stem: A Snowflake table has a CLUSTER BY (region, sale_year) key. After heavy DML (many INSERTs and DELETEs), the Average Depth (AD) of the table rises to 8 and the Average Overlap Depth (AOD) is also very high. What does this indicate and what should be done?

options.text: High Average Depth means the table has too many columns; the clustering key should be removed.
options.explanation: High AD/AOD indicates poor clustering, not good clustering.

options.text: The table needs to be vacuumed using VACUUM TABLE to compact micro-partitions.
options.explanation: VACUUM TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command — Snowflake uses RECLUSTER for clustering maintenance.

options.text: The table is optimally clustered; high Average Depth means data is well-consolidated.
options.explanation: Average Depth is a clustering metric, not related to column count.

options.text: The table has become stale/poorly clustered; a RECLUSTER operation should be triggered (automatic clustering will handle it if enabled).
options.explanation: High Average Depth and high Average Overlap Depth (AOD) indicate that the table's data is no longer well-ordered relative to the clustering key — many micro-partitions overlap in the key column ranges. This means partition pruning is ineffective for queries on those columns. If Automatic Clustering is enabled, Snowflake will eventually recluster the table in the background. If not, a manual RECLUSTER operation can be triggered.

questions.stem: A query that aggregates 1 billion rows is running on a Medium warehouse. The query profile shows significant 'bytes spilled to remote storage'. What is the MOST effective remediation?

options.text: Reduce the AUTO_SUSPEND threshold.
options.explanation: Search Optimization Service targets selective lookup queries, not aggregations that spill.

options.text: Rewrite the query to use a subquery instead of GROUP BY.
options.explanation: AUTO_SUSPEND affects idle billing, not query memory.

options.text: Increase the virtual warehouse size to give the query more memory.
options.explanation: Spilling to remote storage (the worst form of spill) occurs when in-memory and local SSD are both insufficient for intermediate aggregation data. Increasing the warehouse size provides more memory per node, allowing more of the intermediate aggregation data to stay in RAM and eliminating or reducing remote spill.

options.text: Enable the Search Optimization Service on the aggregated table.
options.explanation: GROUP BY is not the cause of spilling — the volume of data being aggregated is. Rewriting to a subquery does not reduce memory requirements.

questions.stem: An application developer has a stream `orders_stream` on the `orders` table. The `orders` table has a Time Travel retention period of 1 day. The stream has not been consumed for 3 days. What is the state of the stream?

options.text: The stream is paused and will resume automatically when consumed.
options.explanation: Stale streams are not paused — they are in an invalid state that requires recreation.

options.text: The stream is stale but can be reset to the current timestamp using ALTER STREAM.
options.explanation: Stale streams do not self-trim to the available Time Travel window.

options.text: The stream is stale and must be recreated to capture new changes.
options.explanation: A stream becomes stale when its offset falls behind the Time Travel retention period of the source table. If the stream was not consumed for 3 days but the table only has 1 day of Time Travel, the stream's offset position is now beyond the available Time Travel window. The stream is stale and the historical change data it was tracking is no longer accessible. The stream must be dropped and recreated to resume CDC.

options.text: The stream only contains the last 24 hours of changes, discarding earlier ones.
options.explanation: There is no ALTER STREAM command to reset a stale stream's offset.

questions.stem: Which virtual warehouse size is the SMALLEST available in Snowflake?

options.text: X-Small (XS)
options.explanation: X-Small (XS) is the smallest virtual warehouse size in Snowflake, consuming 1 credit per hour.

options.text: Nano
options.explanation: Small is the next size up (2 credits/hr). C and D are wrong because Micro and Nano are not valid Snowflake warehouse sizes.

options.text: Micro
Small (S)

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider shares a database using a Data Exchange. Which TWO actions can the data CONSUMER perform with the shared database? (Select TWO)

options.text: Run INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statements on the shared tables.
options.explanation: Consumers cannot zero-copy clone a shared database into their own account — the shared database is read-only and does not belong to the consumer's account.

options.text: Run SELECT queries against the shared tables and views.
options.explanation: and **D** are correct. Consumers of a shared database can query (SELECT) the tables and views in the share. They can also create views in their own database that reference the shared tables — this is a common pattern for adding consumer-side transformations or aliasing without copying data.

options.text: Create a zero-copy clone of the shared database to their own account.
options.explanation: Shared databases are read-only to consumers — INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE are not permitted on provider-owned shared data.

options.text: Create local views in the consumer account that reference the shared tables.

questions.stem: An application developer creates a materialized view `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_daily_sales AS SELECT sale_date, SUM(amount) total FROM sales GROUP BY sale_date`. A new row is inserted into the `sales` table. How does the materialized view stay current?

options.text: Snowflake automatically and incrementally maintains the materialized view as DML changes the base table.
options.explanation: Snowflake materialized views are automatically maintained by Snowflake's background services. When DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) change the base table, Snowflake incrementally updates the materialized view to reflect those changes. This maintenance is transparent to users and happens without manual intervention.

options.text: The materialized view must be manually refreshed using ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REFRESH.
options.explanation: Manual refresh is not required — automatic maintenance is a core feature of Snowflake materialized views.

options.text: The materialized view is invalidated and returns empty results until the next manual refresh.
options.explanation: Maintenance is incremental and event-driven, not hourly full rebuilds.

options.text: The materialized view is rebuilt from scratch every hour on a background schedule.
options.explanation: The materialized view is not invalidated by base table changes.

questions.stem: A warehouse is resized from Medium to Large WHILE queries are actively running on it. What happens to the in-flight queries?

options.text: In-flight queries continue running on the existing Medium-sized compute; the resize applies to subsequently queued or submitted queries.
options.explanation: Snowflake applies warehouse resizes gracefully. Queries that are already running continue on the existing cluster configuration. The new size takes effect for subsequent queries. This means a resize mid-execution does not disrupt running work.

options.text: The resize is rejected until all running queries complete.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not kill in-flight queries on resize.

options.text: The warehouse splits: existing queries finish on Medium nodes while new queries start on Large nodes simultaneously.
options.explanation: Resizes are not blocked by active queries.

options.text: In-flight queries are immediately killed and must be restarted on the new Large warehouse.
options.explanation: The warehouse does not split into concurrent Medium and Large clusters during a resize.

questions.stem: A team is loading a large Parquet file from S3. Which TWO COPY INTO options are specific to or particularly relevant for loading Parquet files? (Select TWO)

options.text: FIELD_DELIMITER = ','
options.explanation: FIELD_DELIMITER is a CSV-specific option — Parquet is a columnar binary format with no field delimiters.

options.text: USE_LOGICAL_TYPE = TRUE
MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE is used when loading Parquet (or other columnar formats) to map source file columns to target table columns by name rather than by position, which is critical for Parquet's named schema. USE_LOGICAL_TYPE = TRUE instructs Snowflake to respect Parquet logical types (e.g., DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP) and convert them to the appropriate Snowflake types rather than loading raw physical values.

options.text: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE
options.explanation: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY applies to JSON files that contain a top-level array.

questions.stem: A user wants to run a query that would normally take 10 minutes on a Medium warehouse. They need it to complete in about 5 minutes. Assuming the query benefits from additional compute (not I/O bound), what should they do?

options.text: Resize the warehouse to a Large size before running the query.
options.explanation: Increasing the warehouse size (vertical scaling) roughly doubles the compute resources, which can approximately halve the runtime for compute-bound queries. A Large warehouse has twice the compute nodes of a Medium warehouse.

options.text: Enable multi-cluster warehouses.
options.explanation: The result cache only helps if the query was previously run with no underlying data changes.

options.text: Reduce the AUTO_SUSPEND to 30 seconds.
options.explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses add additional clusters for concurrency (more users/queries simultaneously), not to speed up a single query.

options.text: Enable the query result cache.
options.explanation: AUTO_SUSPEND affects idle billing, not query execution speed.

questions.stem: An application developer creates a sequence and uses it in an INSERT statement: `INSERT INTO orders (id, product) SELECT orders_seq.NEXTVAL, product FROM staging`. What happens if two concurrent sessions insert rows simultaneously using the same sequence?

options.text: One session blocks until the other completes to ensure sequential values.
options.explanation: Sequences do not generate duplicate values across concurrent sessions.

options.text: Sequences are session-scoped, so each session has its own independent counter starting from 1.
options.explanation: Sequences do not cause blocking — concurrent access is one of their key design properties.

options.text: Snowflake guarantees that each session receives unique, non-overlapping sequence values.
options.explanation: Snowflake sequences are designed for concurrent use — multiple sessions can call NEXTVAL simultaneously and each will receive unique values without collision. Snowflake guarantees uniqueness but not strict monotonic ordering across concurrent sessions (values may not be sequential, but they will be unique).

options.text: Both sessions generate duplicate sequence values, causing primary key violations.
options.explanation: Sequences are account/schema-scoped objects, not session-scoped.

questions.stem: A table has a VARIANT column `payload` containing: `{"orders": [{"id": 1, "item": "pen"}, {"id": 2, "item": "book"}]}`. Which query returns each order as a separate row with columns `order_id` and `item_name`?

options.text: SELECT GET_PATH(payload, 'orders[*].id'), GET_PATH(payload, 'orders[*].item') FROM events;
options.explanation: Colon notation on an array without flattening returns the entire array, not individual elements.

options.text: SELECT UNNEST(payload:orders) FROM events;
options.explanation: GET_PATH with array wildcard `[*]` is not valid Snowflake GET_PATH syntax and would not produce multiple rows.

options.text: SELECT payload:orders:id, payload:orders:item FROM events;
options.explanation: UNNEST is not a Snowflake function — FLATTEN is the equivalent.

options.text: SELECT f.value:id::NUMBER AS order_id, f.value:item::STRING AS item_name FROM events, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => payload:orders) f;
options.explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN on the `orders` array expands each array element into a row, and `f.value` refers to each element (a JSON object). Extracting `:id` and `:item` with appropriate type casts gives the desired result set.

questions.stem: A company implements SAML 2.0 Single Sign-On (SSO) with Snowflake. Which role in Snowflake is responsible for configuring the SAML identity provider settings at the account level?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: Configuring SAML 2.0 SSO at the account level (setting `SAML_IDENTITY_PROVIDER` and `SSO_LOGIN_PAGE`) requires ACCOUNTADMIN privileges, as these are account-level parameters.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: While SECURITYADMIN handles user/role management, configuring the SAML IDP connection at the account level requires ACCOUNTADMIN.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: SYSADMIN manages database objects, not account-level authentication settings.

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: PUBLIC has no administrative privileges.

questions.stem: A company needs to run two separate workloads simultaneously: a heavy ETL process and interactive BI queries. Both workloads must not impact each other's performance. What is the BEST Snowflake approach?

options.text: Schedule the ETL to run after business hours so it does not conflict with BI.
options.explanation: A shared warehouse means both workloads compete for the same concurrency slots and memory.

options.text: Use a single 4X-Large warehouse shared between both workloads.
options.explanation: Scheduling avoidance is an operational workaround, not an architectural solution.

options.text: Enable multi-cluster mode on a single warehouse to split the workloads.
options.explanation: Multi-cluster is designed to scale one warehouse type for concurrent identical workloads, not to separate different workload types.

options.text: Create two separate virtual warehouses — one for ETL and one for BI queries.
options.explanation: Snowflake's architecture allows multiple virtual warehouses to operate independently on the same underlying storage. Creating separate warehouses for ETL and BI provides complete workload isolation — each warehouse has dedicated compute resources and neither can starve the other.

questions.stem: Which object types can a Snowflake data provider add to a share? (Choose the BEST answer)

options.text: Only tables
options.explanation: Secure views and secure UDFs can also be added to shares.

options.text: Tables, regular views, and stored procedures
options.explanation: Tables can be shared directly.

options.text: Only secure views (tables cannot be shared directly)
options.explanation: Regular (non-secure) views cannot be added to shares — only secure views — and stored procedures cannot be shared directly.

options.text: Tables, secure views, and secure UDFs
options.explanation: A Snowflake share can include tables, secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs. Secure views are often used to share a subset of a table or join result while hiding the underlying table definition.

questions.stem: A company has a Snowflake account with sensitive PII data. They want to allow a partner to run aggregate analytics on this data without either party seeing the other's raw data. Which Snowflake feature is designed for this use case?

options.text: A secure view shared via a standard data share.
options.explanation: A zero-copy clone creates a copy accessible to the other party, exposing raw data.

options.text: Zero-copy clone shared between the two accounts.
options.explanation: A secure view still shares the underlying data directly — the consumer can see all rows the view exposes.

options.text: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms.
options.explanation: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms are purpose-built for privacy-preserving collaboration. They allow multiple parties to contribute data and run predefined analyses without either party directly accessing the other's raw data. Aggregate results are returned, but PII is never exposed across the boundary.

options.text: Cross-account database replication with row-level masking.
options.explanation: Replication + masking still makes the replicated data available, just with column masking.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake's schema detection for semi-structured data are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.text: GENERATE_COLUMN_LIST returns a column definition string from INFER_SCHEMA output that can be used in CREATE TABLE statements.
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. INFER_SCHEMA is a Snowflake table function that reads staged files (Parquet, ORC, Avro, JSON with STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY) and returns the inferred column names and data types without loading the data into a table first. GENERATE_COLUMN_LIST is a helper function that formats the INFER_SCHEMA output into a usable column definition string for CREATE TABLE or COPY INTO statements.

options.text: INFER_SCHEMA can automatically detect column names and types from staged Parquet, ORC, Avro, and JSON files.
Schema detection using INFER_SCHEMA works only for CSV files.
options.explanation: INFER_SCHEMA does not work with CSV — CSV files do not carry embedded schema information.

options.text: INFER_SCHEMA requires the files to be loaded into a Snowflake table before the schema can be detected.
options.explanation: INFER_SCHEMA reads files directly from the stage, before any table load.

questions.stem: A data provider wants to share a view of customer data that should hide the underlying SQL logic (table joins and business rules) from the consumer. Which Snowflake object should be shared?

options.text: A secure view created with CREATE SECURE VIEW.
options.explanation: A SECURE VIEW in Snowflake hides the view's definition (SQL logic) from the consumer. When a view is marked as SECURE, the SHOW VIEWS and GET_DDL() output does not expose the view body to non-owner roles. Only secure views can be added to data shares.

options.text: A materialized view created with CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW.
options.explanation: Regular views can be shared only in limited contexts, and they do not hide the view definition.

options.text: A stored procedure that returns a result set.
options.explanation: While materialized views can be made secure, standard materialized views are not automatically secure and the question asks about hiding SQL logic — secure view is the direct answer.

options.text: A regular view with GRANT SELECT on the view.
options.explanation: Stored procedures cannot be added to shares.

questions.stem: A company on the Standard edition asks which features are NOT available to them. Which TWO features are exclusive to Enterprise edition and above? (Select TWO)

options.text: Data sharing with reader accounts
options.explanation: Fail-Safe (7-day recovery period) is available on all editions for standard tables.

options.text: Multi-cluster warehouses
Fail-Safe data recovery
options.explanation: Data sharing and reader accounts are available on Standard edition — data sharing is a core Snowflake feature not gated to Enterprise.

options.text: Time Travel retention period of up to 90 days
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. Time Travel retention of up to 90 days (Standard is capped at 1 day) and multi-cluster warehouses are both Enterprise edition features not available on Standard.

questions.stem: An application developer creates a table: `CREATE TABLE events (id INT, raw VARIANT)`. They load JSON data with STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE. Later they run: `SELECT TYPEOF(raw:amount) FROM events`. Some rows return 'REAL', some return 'INTEGER', and some return 'NULL_VALUE'. What explains this?

options.text: REAL and INTEGER both mean numeric; the difference is a display artifact with no functional significance.
options.explanation: VARIANT explicitly allows mixed types — it is a feature, not an error.

options.text: TYPEOF() returns 'NULL_VALUE' only for rows where the raw column itself is NULL.
options.explanation: REAL and INTEGER are distinct type representations that can affect arithmetic and comparisons.

options.text: Snowflake requires all VARIANT keys to have the same type; the mixed types indicate a load error.
options.explanation: 'NULL_VALUE' specifically refers to a JSON null value or missing key, not a NULL VARIANT column.

options.text: VARIANT columns are schema-less — different rows can have different types for the same key path, and TYPEOF() reflects the actual stored type per row.
options.explanation: VARIANT is schema-less in Snowflake. Different rows can store different JSON types for the same key path — some rows may have `amount` as a decimal (stored as REAL), others as an integer (stored as INTEGER), and others may be missing the key entirely (TYPEOF returns 'NULL_VALUE' when the key is absent or the value is JSON null). TYPEOF() reports the actual type of the value in each row's VARIANT.

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Data Marketplace?

options.text: An internal Snowflake tool for managing data warehouse assets and schemas.
options.explanation: The Marketplace is an external data commerce platform, not an internal schema management tool.

options.text: A curated online catalog where providers publish live data products that consumers can discover and access directly in their Snowflake accounts.
options.explanation: The Snowflake Marketplace (formerly Data Marketplace) is a data commerce platform where third-party data providers publish live, ready-to-query datasets. Consumers can discover, request access to, and instantly mount these datasets into their own Snowflake account without any ETL or data movement — the data is shared live using Snowflake's native sharing architecture.

options.text: A file transfer service for sharing large datasets between Snowflake accounts.
options.explanation: It has nothing to do with credit purchasing.

options.text: A third-party app store for purchasing Snowflake compute credits at discounted rates.
options.explanation: No files are transferred — data sharing is live and zero-copy.

questions.stem: An application developer notices that a query runs in 10 seconds the first time but only 1 second on the second run with no data changes. The virtual warehouse remained active between runs. Which performance feature is MOST likely responsible for the speedup?

options.text: Automatic Query Optimization (AQE) rewriting the query on the second run.
options.explanation: AQE performs automatic plan adjustment, not a separate optimization between runs.

options.text: Search Optimization Service pre-indexing the table between the two runs.
options.explanation: Search Optimization Service works on selective point-lookup patterns, not general scan speed.

options.text: The result cache returning the precomputed answer.
The local disk cache (data cache) on the warehouse nodes retaining previously read micro-partitions in memory/SSD.
options.explanation: The virtual warehouse's local disk cache (also called the data cache or remote disk cache) stores micro-partitions that were read from cloud storage during the first query. On the second run, the warehouse reads the same partitions from local SSD cache instead of fetching them from remote object storage, which is significantly faster. The result cache would return results in sub-millisecond time (essentially instant), not 1 second, so 1 second suggests compute still ran but faster due to cached data.

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes the Economy scaling policy for multi-cluster virtual warehouses?

options.text: The Economy policy prevents Snowflake from ever removing clusters once they are added.
options.explanation: Immediately provisioning on any queued query is the Standard (not Economy) scaling policy behavior.

options.text: The Economy policy disables automatic scaling and requires manual cluster management.
options.explanation: Both Standard and Economy are automatic scaling policies.

options.text: Snowflake adds a new cluster as soon as any query is queued.
options.explanation: Both policies remove clusters when demand drops.

options.text: Snowflake adds a new cluster only when it estimates that the current cluster(s) will be busy for at least 6 minutes.
options.explanation: The Economy scaling policy is conservative — Snowflake waits until it predicts the queued load will keep an additional cluster busy for at least 6 minutes before provisioning it. This avoids spinning up clusters for brief spikes, trading some query latency for lower credit consumption.

questions.stem: An application developer uses `SELECT PARSE_JSON('{"score": 95}'):score`. What does this expression return?

options.text: The integer 95 as a NUMBER data type.
options.explanation: The result is VARIANT, not NUMBER, without an explicit cast.

options.text: An error because colon notation requires a column reference, not an inline PARSE_JSON call.
options.explanation: No cast to STRING is applied.

options.text: The VARIANT value 95 (number within a VARIANT container).
options.explanation: PARSE_JSON() parses a JSON string and returns a VARIANT. Applying :score (colon notation) to the VARIANT navigates to the 'score' key and returns the value as a VARIANT. The result is a VARIANT containing the numeric value 95, not a native NUMBER. To get a NUMBER, you would need to cast: `PARSE_JSON('{"score": 95}'):score::NUMBER`.

options.text: The string '95' as a VARCHAR.
options.explanation: Colon notation is valid on any VARIANT expression, not just column references.

questions.stem: An application developer uses `TRY_CAST(data:price::STRING AS NUMBER)` on a VARIANT column where some rows have `price` as a string like `"N/A"`. What is returned for those rows?

options.text: The original string value 'N/A' is returned unchanged.
options.explanation: TRY_CAST specifically avoids raising errors — that is the purpose of the TRY_ prefix (use CAST or :: for error-raising behavior).

options.text: 0 is returned as a default numeric value for failed casts.
options.explanation: TRY_CAST always returns the target type or NULL — it never returns the original value in the original type.

options.text: An error is raised for each row where the cast fails.
options.explanation: TRY_CAST returns NULL, not 0, on failure.

options.text: NULL is returned for rows where the cast to NUMBER fails.
options.explanation: TRY_CAST is the safe casting function in Snowflake that returns NULL instead of raising an error when a type conversion fails. For rows where `data:price` is 'N/A' (which cannot be converted to a NUMBER), TRY_CAST returns NULL.

questions.stem: What Snowflake data type is used to store semi-structured data such as JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, or XML?

options.text: VARIANT
options.explanation: VARIANT is Snowflake's native data type for storing semi-structured data. A VARIANT column can hold any JSON, XML, Avro, ORC, or Parquet document up to 16 MB in size. Snowflake automatically parses and stores it in an optimized internal format.

options.text: BLOB
options.explanation: BLOB is not a Snowflake native type for semi-structured data.

options.text: TEXT
options.explanation: TEXT (or VARCHAR) stores plain text strings without structured parsing.

options.text: STRUCT
options.explanation: STRUCT is not a Snowflake data type — though OBJECT is related.

questions.stem: A virtual warehouse is consistently showing long query queue times during business hours. Queries are waiting for other queries to finish before executing. What is the MOST likely solution?

options.text: Enable the Search Optimization Service on all queried tables.
options.explanation: Increasing warehouse size adds more compute per query (better for slow individual queries) but does NOT increase the number of concurrent queries a warehouse can handle.

options.text: Enable multi-cluster warehouses to handle concurrent queries.
options.explanation: Query queuing is a concurrency problem, not a compute-per-query problem. Multi-cluster warehouses address concurrency by adding additional clusters to handle simultaneous queries, eliminating the queue.

options.text: Increase the warehouse size from Medium to Large.
options.explanation: Search Optimization Service improves individual query performance for selective lookups but does not solve queue buildup from concurrency.

options.text: Reduce the AUTO_SUSPEND threshold to 30 seconds.
options.explanation: Reducing auto-suspend affects idle credit usage, not query concurrency.

questions.stem: A Snowflake table has a VARIANT column containing JSON data. An application developer runs `SELECT v:name::STRING FROM json_table WHERE v:status::STRING = 'active'`. Snowflake runs this query efficiently without scanning all rows. What Snowflake optimization enables this?

options.text: Search Optimization Service must be enabled for VARIANT path pruning.
options.explanation: Basic VARIANT path pruning is built into micro-partition metadata — the Search Optimization Service provides additional optimization for highly selective equality lookups.

options.text: Snowflake converts all VARIANT data to structured columns at query time using schema-on-read.
options.explanation: Clustering on VARIANT columns is not automatic.

options.text: Snowflake stores metadata (min/max per VARIANT path) within each micro-partition, enabling pruning on VARIANT paths just as on structured columns.
options.explanation: Snowflake's micro-partition metadata includes statistics for commonly accessed VARIANT paths (virtual columns). This allows Snowflake to prune micro-partitions based on VARIANT path predicates similarly to regular column predicates, without loading the full JSON from all partitions.

options.text: Automatic clustering on VARIANT columns is applied by default.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not convert VARIANT to structured columns — it uses the stored VARIANT with path-level metadata.

questions.stem: A new custom role `REPORTING` is created and granted to a user. For the user to use this role to query a table in `mydb.analytics`, which privileges are required at a minimum?

options.text: USAGE on the database and schema only
